title: Death Verification & Certification
oneLiner: Confirming irreversible cessation of life and completing the legal medical certificate of cause of death — a critical skill for every junior doctor.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
- **Classic vignette:** You are bleeped to a side room on the ward. The nursing staff say Mr. Jones, 78 years old with known ischaemic heart disease and COPD, was found unresponsive 10 minutes ago. On arrival, he is warm to touch, no respiratory effort, no carotid or femoral pulse. Pupils are fixed and dilated. You confirm with a Doppler or stethoscope — no heart sounds, no breath sounds. There is no response to painful stimuli. The nursing staff have already placed him in a supine position. You document the time of examination, findings, and begin the certification process.
- **Dissecting the vignette — pattern clues:**
  - "Found unresponsive 10 minutes ago" — suggests recent death; may still be warm, no early signs of decomposition
  - "Warm to touch" — within first few hours; later will become cold (algor mortis)
  - "No carotid/femoral pulse" — key confirmatory sign; you must palpate central pulses for at least 30 seconds
  - "Pupils fixed and dilated" — loss of brainstem reflexes; confirm with penlight (direct and consensual)
  - "No response to painful stimuli" — suprapubic pressure or trapezius squeeze; absence of motor response
  - "Doppler confirms no heart sounds" — ancillary test if uncertain; mandatory in some trusts
  - "Supine position" — needed for examination; also relevant for post-mortem hypostasis (livor mortis) later
- **Atypical presentations:**
  - **Drug overdose (e.g., opioids, benzodiazepines):** Patient may be deeply unconscious with absent pulses and respiratory effort, but pupils may be pinpoint (miotic) rather than dilated. Do not mistake for death — give naloxone trial if suspected. This is a "near-death" mimic.
  - **Hypothermic death:** Patient very cold, no palpable pulse, pupils fixed, but can be revived with prolonged CPR and rewarming. The dictum "no one is dead until warm and dead" applies. Do not verify death until core temperature >32°C.
  - **Brainstem death on ICU:** Patient ventilated, no motor responses, absent brainstem reflexes — but heart still beating. This is a different process (brainstem death testing) and should not be confused with cardiorespiratory death. The pattern is a ventilated patient with no spontaneous effort.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
- **Plain-language definition:** Death verification is the medical procedure of confirming that a person has permanently lost all circulatory, respiratory, and brainstem function. Certification is the subsequent legal documentation of the cause(s) of death, based on medical knowledge and any available post-mortem information.
- **Why this matters clinically:**
  - **Frequency:** Every junior doctor will perform death verification dozens of times per year — it is the single most common "procedure" on medical wards.
  - **Stakes:** Incorrect verification can lead to premature burial or missed resuscitation (if reversible cause overlooked). Incorrect certification can mislead public health statistics, cause medico-legal problems, or delay the cremation/burial process.
  - **Common pitfalls:** Failing to check for reversible causes, not documenting signs, certifying without patient knowledge, using ambiguous terms like "old age" or "cardiac arrest" incorrectly, and not involving the coroner when required.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
- **Story of death:**
  - Death occurs when the heart stops pumping (circulatory arrest) or when the brainstem ceases to drive breathing (respiratory arrest), typically leading to cessation of oxygen delivery to tissues. Within seconds, consciousness is lost. Within minutes, brainstem reflexes are lost. Without intervention, irreversible anoxic damage occurs.
- **Why each clinical sign happens (mechanism -> sign):**
  - **No palpable pulse:** Cessation of cardiac output → absent pulse pressure → no palpable carotid, femoral, or radial pulses. Absence of heart sounds on auscultation (for at least 30 seconds) confirms asystole or pulseless electrical activity.
  - **No respiratory effort:** Ischaemia of the medullary respiratory centre → no spontaneous breaths. Check for chest rise over 1 minute.
  - **Fixed and dilated pupils:** Loss of parasympathetic and sympathetic innervation to the iris due to brainstem ischaemia → pupils become mid-dilated (7–10 mm) and unreactive. Note that drugs (e.g., atropine, opioids) can alter pupil size.
  - **Absence of response to pain:** Brainstem and cortical death → no motor or reflexive response to noxious stimuli. Trapezius squeeze tests spinal accessory nerve (CN XI and C1–C4). Suprapubic pressure stimulates sacral nerves.
  - **Algor mortis (cooling):** No metabolic heat production → body cools towards ambient temperature at ~0.5–1°C per hour. Forehead and abdomen cool slower than extremities.
  - **Rigor mortis (stiffening):** After ~2–6 hours, depletion of ATP prevents actin-myosin dissociation → muscles stiffen. Starts in small muscles (jaw, eyelids) then large (limbs). Resolves after 24–48 hours.
  - **Livor mortis (lividity):** Blood pooling in dependent capillaries due to gravity → purplish discoloration over lowermost skin. Blanches initially (first few hours) then becomes fixed (non-blanching) after ~4–6 hours.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
- **Natural causes (most common):**
  - Cardiovascular (ischaemic heart disease, heart failure, arrhythmia) — "cardiac arrest unwitnessed"
  - Respiratory (pneumonia, COPD, pulmonary embolism) — "dyspnoea, then collapse"
  - Neurological (stroke, intracranial haemorrhage, seizure) — "sudden headache, coma"
  - Sepsis — "fever, hypotension, multi-organ failure"
  - Malignancy — "cachexia, advanced cancer"
- **Unnatural causes (coroner involvement mandatory):**
  - Trauma (road traffic accident, fall, assault) — "suspicious injury"
  - Self-harm / suicide — "hanging, drowning, overdose"
  - Poisoning (drug overdose, CO poisoning) — "confusion, then coma"
  - Homicide — "unexplained wounds"
  - Anaesthetic / surgical misadventure — "post-operative collapse"
- **Iatrogenic / medication-related:** Anticoagulant-induced bleed, chemotherapy toxicity, contrast nephropathy — "recent intervention/treatment"
- **Unknown / sudden unexpected:** Sudden adult death syndrome (SADS), arrhythmia, occult sepsis — "brought in dead, no history"
- **Highlight most common cause:** Ischaemic heart disease (in the UK) — responsible for ~25% of all deaths. Often presents as "found dead in bed" or "sudden collapse".
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
- **Diagnostic criteria:** The concept of death is clinical — no single laboratory test confirms death. The Royal College of Physicians (UK) guidelines require:
  - Absence of circulation for at least 30 seconds (pulse, heart sounds)
  - Absence of respiratory effort for at least 1 minute
  - Absence of brainstem reflexes (pupillary light, corneal, gag, cough, oculovestibular) — only for brainstem death testing (ventilated patients)
- **For each "test":**
  - **Pulse palpation (carotid/femoral):** Shows no detectable pulse. Why: confirms circulatory arrest. Interpret: if absent for >30 seconds, death is likely. Pitfall: cardiovert with defibrillator if rhythm is shockable? No — in death verification, no resuscitation should be started; but if you suspect arrest, you should start CPR, not verify death.
  - **Auscultation (heart and lung fields):** No heart sounds or breath sounds. Why: confirm no cardiac or respiratory activity. Interpret: 30 seconds of silence.
  - **Pupil examination (penlight):** Fixed and dilated. Why: brainstem death. Interpret: no direct or consensual response.
  - **Pain response (trapezius squeeze, supraorbital pressure, suprapubic pressure):** No grimace, movement, or eye opening. Why: cortical and brainstem death.
  - **Capnography (if available on ICU):** End-tidal CO2 = 0 mmHg. Why: confirm no perfusion of the lungs. Interpret: no metabolic CO2 production.
  - **ECG (if confusion):** Asystole or agonal rhythm. Why: rule out fine VF or PEA. Pitfall: PEA can have no pulse — treat with CPR, not death verification. Only use ECG if you are unsure about the rhythm.
- **Pattern-recognition:**
  - Single finding that **confirms** death: Absence of central pulse >30 seconds + no heart sounds + no respiratory efforts + fixed pupils = clinical death. No single test alone suffices — the combination is diagnostic.
  - Single finding that **rules out** death: Any pupillary reaction, any gasping, a palpable pulse, or response to pain. If any present, do not certify.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
- **Immediate (first hour):**
  - **Confirm death** using the above signs. Wear gloves. Position patient supine, remove dentures if needed (for certification). Check for alarm signs: if there is any doubt (e.g., suspected hypothermia, overdose, poisoning) — **do not certify**. Attempt CPR if the arrest is unwitnessed and patient is potentially salvageable.
  - **Document findings in notes:** Date, time, patient details, statement “I confirm that (name) died at (time) on (date).” Sign, print name, GMC number. Use the hospital death verification policy.
  - **Inform nursing staff and family** with sensitivity. Use phrases like “I’m sorry, (name) has died.” Offer time for family to sit with the deceased.
- **Ward (24–72 hours):**
  - **Complete the Medical Certificate of Cause of Death (MCCD):** Write legibly in black permanent ink. Part I: immediate cause (e.g., 1a Acute myocardial infarction, 1b Coronary heart disease, 1c Hypertension). Part II: other contributory conditions (e.g., COPD, diabetes). Do not use abbreviations. Do not write "cardiac arrest" as final cause — it is the mechanism, not the underlying pathology.
  - **Arrange for coroner referral if required** (see escalation). If not, give MCCD to the family/funeral director. Record that MCCD was issued.
  - **Check for pacemaker/ICD:** If present, arrange deactivation (usually by cardiology or mortuary staff) to prevent shock to mortuary staff.
- **Long-term:**
  - **If post-mortem is required,** delay cremation until coroner gives permission. Arrange viewing of body for family if desired.
  - **Follow up with GP** for death summary to updated medical records.
- **What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
  - ❌ **Do not certify if you find a reversible cause** (hypothermia, overdose, snake bite, extreme hypoglycaemia). Start resuscitation.
  - ❌ **Do not write “old age”** unless the person is over 80 and there is no other identifiable cause (avoid this if possible).
  - ❌ **Do not write “cardiac arrest”** as the underlying cause of death — it is the terminal event. Always state the disease causing the arrest.
  - ❌ **Do not certify if death is unnatural** (suspicious, trauma, suicide, etc.) without informing the coroner first.
  - ❌ **Do not leave the body in a public area** — transfer to mortuary or quiet room with dignity.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
**Top mimics of cardiorespiratory death:**
| Feature | **Death Verification Candidate** | **Severe Hypothermia** | **Opioid Overdose** | **Brainstem Death (ICU)** |
|---------|----------------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| **Core temperature** | Normal / cooling | <32°C | Normal | Usually normal (ventilated) |
| **Pulse** | Absent for >30s | May be undetectable but present (bradycardia) | May be present (brady/arrhythmia) | Present (heart beating) |
| **Respiratory effort** | Absent for >1 min | Very slow ( < 6/min) or absent | Apnoea (may be shallow) | Absent (ventilator dependent) |
| **Pupils** | Fixed and dilated | Dilated but may react (cold mydriasis) | Pinpoint (miotic) | Fixed and often mid-dilated |
| **Response to pain** | None | May have subtle reflex | None (if deep coma) | None (but spinal reflexes may occur) |
| **ECG** | Asystole or PEA | J wave (Osborn wave), bradycardia | Sinus rhythm or other | Heart rate normal (e.g., sinus) |
| **Key differentiator** | No circulation, no brainstem function | Core temp <32°C + history of exposure | History + pinpoint pupils + bradypnoea + response to naloxone | Clinically brain dead but heart beating; requires specific testing protocol |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Missed reversible cause (e.g., opioid overdose):** Patient incorrectly certified as dead → no naloxone given → actual death occurs. **Signs:** Pinpoint pupils, needle marks, shallow breathing. **Action:** Always check for drug history and pupil size. If in doubt, do not certify; start CPR and give naloxone if suspected.
- **Certification of a live person (very rare):** The patient later regains consciousness (e.g., after a seizure, hypoglycaemia). **Signs:** sudden movement, gasping, pulse returns. **Action:** immediate call for help, do not certify until fully evaluated. Document incident and senior review.
- **Incorrect cause of death on MCCD:** May lead to coroner's investigation, distressed family, or legal proceedings. **Prevention:** Always check medical records, discuss with senior if uncertain, and never guess.
- **Failure to refer to coroner when required:** Leads to delay, potential prosecution for concealing a death. **Examples:** Industrial disease, post-surgical death, suicide. **Action:** Know the list of coroner-notifiable deaths (e.g., all unnatural, sudden unexpected, within 24h of admission, during an operation).
- **Late-presentation pattern – the unexamined death:** If a patient is found dead after hours of being unwitnessed, signs of decomposition (greenish discoloration, bloating, putrid odour) are present. The examiner must still document signs of death but should not delay certification if clear. However, if there is any suspicion of foul play, do not touch the body further and contact police.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
- **Mnemonic for death verification steps: "DRS ABC" (modified)**
  - **D** – Danger (gloves, privacy, environment)
  - **R** – Response (vocal, painful stimuli)
  - **S** – Send for help if needed (but here you are confirming death)
  - **A** – Airway (check patency? No — not needed once death confirmed, but before confirmation you might clear obstruction if you suspect reversible)
  - **B** – Breathing (look, listen, feel for 1 min)
  - **C** – Circulation (palpate carotid/femoral for 30s)
  Add: **P** – Pupils (light), **R** – Reflexes (pain), **E** – ECG (if doubt)
- **Another mnemonic for what to document: "TIME-LIFE"**
  - **T** – Time of examination
  - **I** – Identity confirmed
  - **M** – Mechanism (signs of death)
  - **E** – Environment (privacy, dignity maintained)
  - **L** – Legal (coroner involved? )
  - **I** – Instructions (for funeral director)
  - **F** – Family informed
  - **E** – Education (offer conversation)
- **Clinical pearls:**
  - **The “30/1” rule:** Pulseless for 30s + apnoea for 1 min + fixed pupils = clinically dead. No need for EEG.
  - **Hot tip:** If you are unsure about pupil dilation, look for a corneal reflex. If absent, it’s a strong confirmatory sign (not always present in deep coma but reliable).
  - **Gasping:** Terminal gasps (agonal breathing) can occur for several minutes after cardiac arrest—this does NOT mean alive. Do not mistake for respiratory effort. Confirm with palpation of pulse; if absent, it’s a sign of death, not life.
  - **Brady-arrhythmia in hypothermia:** Do not attempt to verify death until rewarmed. The saying: “You are not dead until you are warm and dead.”
  - **Don't forget the pacemaker:** Always document presence or absence. If present, mortuary staff must deactivate it to avoid shocking staff.
  - **The “death certificate” is a legal document:** Write in permanent black ink, no correction fluid. If you make an error, draw a single line through the mistake and initial.
  - **Coroner referral checklist:** Unnatural, sudden (<24h from onset), unknown cause, during treatment, related to occupation, suicide, during pregnancy, or in custody. Always phone coroner's officer if unsure.
- **Common exam / ward mistakes:**
  - ❌ Writing "cardiac arrest" as 1a cause of death.
  - ❌ Forgetting to check for a micro-pacemaker (cardiac resynchronisation therapy) — leads to defibrillator shocks in mortuary.
  - ❌ Not documenting the time of death accurately (must match the time of verification, not time of last seen alive).
  - ❌ Certifying death without examining the patient (e.g., relying on nursing report alone — never do this).
  - ❌ Using terms like "old age" or "frailty" without a solid history — this is increasingly scrutinised.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior doctor (Registrar/Consultant) when:**
  - Cause of death is unclear (no previous diagnosis, no known history)
  - You suspect an unnatural cause (trauma, poisoning, suicide)
  - Patient died within 24 hours of admission to hospital
  - Patient died in surgery or recovery
  - Patient died while under anaesthesia
  - Patient was a prisoner or in police custody
  - Family disputes cause of death
  - You are uncertain about the completion of the MCCD
- **Call coroner (via bereavement officer or direct) when:**
  - Any of the above and not yet seen by a consultant; or the death is reportable by statute
  - If the patient had an industrial disease (e.g., asbestosis, coal worker's pneumoconiosis)
  - If the death is a suicide (suspected or confirmed)
  - If the death was violent or unnatural (fall, RTA, stabbing)
  - If the body is to be buried abroad (unless MCCD already accepted)
- **Specific thresholds for when to involve ICU/crash team:**
  - **NEWS2** – not directly applicable for dead patient, but if you are in doubt about verification (e.g., suspected reversible cause) and the patient has a score of ≥5, initiate resuscitation call.
  - **Lactate** – not part of death verification. Use clinical signs.
  - **GCS** – if any motor or eye response >2, do not verify. If GCS 3 with no brainstem reflexes, brainstem death testing may be appropriate (but on ICU).
  - **Caveat:** Never call a crash team for a patient who is clearly dead with irreversible signs (rigor mortis, algor mortis, dependent lividity) — that is inappropriate. But if in any doubt, err on the side of calling for help.

## Death Verification & Certification
**One-liner:** Confirming irreversible cessation of life and completing the legal medical certificate of cause of death — a critical skill for every junior doctor.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
- **Classic vignette:** You are bleeped to a side room on the ward. The nursing staff say Mr. Jones, 78 years old with known ischaemic heart disease and COPD, was found unresponsive 10 minutes ago. On arrival, he is warm to touch, no respiratory effort, no carotid or femoral pulse. Pupils are fixed and dilated. You confirm with a Doppler or stethoscope — no heart sounds, no breath sounds. There is no response to painful stimuli. The nursing staff have already placed him in a supine position. You document the time of examination, findings, and begin the certification process.
- **Dissecting the vignette — pattern clues:**
  - "Found unresponsive 10 minutes ago" — suggests recent death; may still be warm, no early signs of decomposition
  - "Warm to touch" — within first few hours; later will become cold (algor mortis)
  - "No carotid/femoral pulse" — key confirmatory sign; you must palpate central pulses for at least 30 seconds
  - "Pupils fixed and dilated" — loss of brainstem reflexes; confirm with penlight (direct and consensual)
  - "No response to painful stimuli" — suprapubic pressure or trapezius squeeze; absence of motor response
  - "Doppler confirms no heart sounds" — ancillary test if uncertain; mandatory in some trusts
  - "Supine position" — needed for examination; also relevant for post-mortem hypostasis (livor mortis) later
- **Atypical presentations:**
  - **Drug overdose (e.g., opioids, benzodiazepines):** Patient may be deeply unconscious with absent pulses and respiratory effort, but pupils may be pinpoint (miotic) rather than dilated. Do not mistake for death — give naloxone trial if suspected. This is a "near-death" mimic.
  - **Hypothermic death:** Patient very cold, no palpable pulse, pupils fixed, but can be revived with prolonged CPR and rewarming. The dictum "no one is dead until warm and dead" applies. Do not verify death until core temperature >32°C.
  - **Brainstem death on ICU:** Patient ventilated, no motor responses, absent brainstem reflexes — but heart still beating. This is a different process (brainstem death testing) and should not be confused with cardiorespiratory death. The pattern is a ventilated patient with no spontaneous effort.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
- **Plain-language definition:** Death verification is the medical procedure of confirming that a person has permanently lost all circulatory, respiratory, and brainstem function. Certification is the subsequent legal documentation of the cause(s) of death, based on medical knowledge and any available post-mortem information.
- **Why this matters clinically:**
  - **Frequency:** Every junior doctor will perform death verification dozens of times per year — it is the single most common "procedure" on medical wards.
  - **Stakes:** Incorrect verification can lead to premature burial or missed resuscitation (if reversible cause overlooked). Incorrect certification can mislead public health statistics, cause medico-legal problems, or delay the cremation/burial process.
  - **Common pitfalls:** Failing to check for reversible causes, not documenting signs, certifying without patient knowledge, using ambiguous terms like "old age" or "cardiac arrest" incorrectly, and not involving the coroner when required.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
- **Story of death:**
  - Death occurs when the heart stops pumping (circulatory arrest) or when the brainstem ceases to drive breathing (respiratory arrest), typically leading to cessation of oxygen delivery to tissues. Within seconds, consciousness is lost. Within minutes, brainstem reflexes are lost. Without intervention, irreversible anoxic damage occurs.
- **Why each clinical sign happens (mechanism -> sign):**
  - **No palpable pulse:** Cessation of cardiac output → absent pulse pressure → no palpable carotid, femoral, or radial pulses. Absence of heart sounds on auscultation (for at least 30 seconds) confirms asystole or pulseless electrical activity.
  - **No respiratory effort:** Ischaemia of the medullary respiratory centre → no spontaneous breaths. Check for chest rise over 1 minute.
  - **Fixed and dilated pupils:** Loss of parasympathetic and sympathetic innervation to the iris due to brainstem ischaemia → pupils become mid-dilated (7–10 mm) and unreactive. Note that drugs (e.g., atropine, opioids) can alter pupil size.
  - **Absence of response to pain:** Brainstem and cortical death → no motor or reflexive response to noxious stimuli. Trapezius squeeze tests spinal accessory nerve (CN XI and C1–C4). Suprapubic pressure stimulates sacral nerves.
  - **Algor mortis (cooling):** No metabolic heat production → body cools towards ambient temperature at ~0.5–1°C per hour. Forehead and abdomen cool slower than extremities.
  - **Rigor mortis (stiffening):** After ~2–6 hours, depletion of ATP prevents actin-myosin dissociation → muscles stiffen. Starts in small muscles (jaw, eyelids) then large (limbs). Resolves after 24–48 hours.
  - **Livor mortis (lividity):** Blood pooling in dependent capillaries due to gravity → purplish discoloration over lowermost skin. Blanches initially (first few hours) then becomes fixed (non-blanching) after ~4–6 hours.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
- **Natural causes (most common):**
  - Cardiovascular (ischaemic heart disease, heart failure, arrhythmia) — "cardiac arrest unwitnessed"
  - Respiratory (pneumonia, COPD, pulmonary embolism) — "dyspnoea, then collapse"
  - Neurological (stroke, intracranial haemorrhage, seizure) — "sudden headache, coma"
  - Sepsis — "fever, hypotension, multi-organ failure"
  - Malignancy — "cachexia, advanced cancer"
- **Unnatural causes (coroner involvement mandatory):**
  - Trauma (road traffic accident, fall, assault) — "suspicious injury"
  - Self-harm / suicide — "hanging, drowning, overdose"
  - Poisoning (drug overdose, CO poisoning) — "confusion, then coma"
  - Homicide — "unexplained wounds"
  - Anaesthetic / surgical misadventure — "post-operative collapse"
- **Iatrogenic / medication-related:** Anticoagulant-induced bleed, chemotherapy toxicity, contrast nephropathy — "recent intervention/treatment"
- **Unknown / sudden unexpected:** Sudden adult death syndrome (SADS), arrhythmia, occult sepsis — "brought in dead, no history"
- **Highlight most common cause:** Ischaemic heart disease (in the UK) — responsible for ~25% of all deaths. Often presents as "found dead in bed" or "sudden collapse".

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
- **Diagnostic criteria:** The concept of death is clinical — no single laboratory test confirms death. The Royal College of Physicians (UK) guidelines require:
  - Absence of circulation for at least 30 seconds (pulse, heart sounds)
  - Absence of respiratory effort for at least 1 minute
  - Absence of brainstem reflexes (pupillary light, corneal, gag, cough, oculovestibular) — only for brainstem death testing (ventilated patients)
- **For each "test":**
  - **Pulse palpation (carotid/femoral):** Shows no detectable pulse. Why: confirms circulatory arrest. Interpret: if absent for >30 seconds, death is likely. Pitfall: cardiovert with defibrillator if rhythm is shockable? No — in death verification, no resuscitation should be started; but if you suspect arrest, you should start CPR, not verify death.
  - **Auscultation (heart and lung fields):** No heart sounds or breath sounds. Why: confirm no cardiac or respiratory activity. Interpret: 30 seconds of silence.
  - **Pupil examination (penlight):** Fixed and dilated. Why: brainstem death. Interpret: no direct or consensual response.
  - **Pain response (trapezius squeeze, supraorbital pressure, suprapubic pressure):** No grimace, movement, or eye opening. Why: cortical and brainstem death.
  - **Capnography (if available on ICU):** End-tidal CO2 = 0 mmHg. Why: confirm no perfusion of the lungs. Interpret: no metabolic CO2 production.
  - **ECG (if confusion):** Asystole or agonal rhythm. Why: rule out fine VF or PEA. Pitfall: PEA can have no pulse — treat with CPR, not death verification. Only use ECG if you are unsure about the rhythm.
- **Pattern-recognition:**
  - Single finding that **confirms** death: Absence of central pulse >30 seconds + no heart sounds + no respiratory efforts + fixed pupils = clinical death. No single test alone suffices — the combination is diagnostic.
  - Single finding that **rules out** death: Any pupillary reaction, any gasping, a palpable pulse, or response to pain. If any present, do not certify.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
- **Immediate (first hour):**
  - **Confirm death** using the above signs. Wear gloves. Position patient supine, remove dentures if needed (for certification). Check for alarm signs: if there is any doubt (e.g., suspected hypothermia, overdose, poisoning) — **do not certify**. Attempt CPR if the arrest is unwitnessed and patient is potentially salvageable.
  - **Document findings in notes:** Date, time, patient details, statement “I confirm that (name) died at (time) on (date).” Sign, print name, GMC number. Use the hospital death verification policy.
  - **Inform nursing staff and family** with sensitivity. Use phrases like “I’m sorry, (name) has died.” Offer time for family to sit with the deceased.
- **Ward (24–72 hours):**
  - **Complete the Medical Certificate of Cause of Death (MCCD):** Write legibly in black permanent ink. Part I: immediate cause (e.g., 1a Acute myocardial infarction, 1b Coronary heart disease, 1c Hypertension). Part II: other contributory conditions (e.g., COPD, diabetes). Do not use abbreviations. Do not write "cardiac arrest" as final cause — it is the mechanism, not the underlying pathology.
  - **Arrange for coroner referral if required** (see escalation). If not, give MCCD to the family/funeral director. Record that MCCD was issued.
  - **Check for pacemaker/ICD:** If present, arrange deactivation (usually by cardiology or mortuary staff) to prevent shock to mortuary staff.
- **Long-term:**
  - **If post-mortem is required,** delay cremation until coroner gives permission. Arrange viewing of body for family if desired.
  - **Follow up with GP** for death summary to updated medical records.
- **What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
  - ❌ **Do not certify if you find a reversible cause** (hypothermia, overdose, snake bite, extreme hypoglycaemia). Start resuscitation.
  - ❌ **Do not write “old age”** unless the person is over 80 and there is no other identifiable cause (avoid this if possible).
  - ❌ **Do not write “cardiac arrest”** as the underlying cause of death — it is the terminal event. Always state the disease causing the arrest.
  - ❌ **Do not certify if death is unnatural** (suspicious, trauma, suicide, etc.) without informing the coroner first.
  - ❌ **Do not leave the body in a public area** — transfer to mortuary or quiet room with dignity.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
**Top mimics of cardiorespiratory death:**
| Feature | **Death Verification Candidate** | **Severe Hypothermia** | **Opioid Overdose** | **Brainstem Death (ICU)** |
|---------|----------------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| **Core temperature** | Normal / cooling | <32°C | Normal | Usually normal (ventilated) |
| **Pulse** | Absent for >30s | May be undetectable but present (bradycardia) | May be present (brady/arrhythmia) | Present (heart beating) |
| **Respiratory effort** | Absent for >1 min | Very slow ( < 6/min) or absent | Apnoea (may be shallow) | Absent (ventilator dependent) |
| **Pupils** | Fixed and dilated | Dilated but may react (cold mydriasis) | Pinpoint (miotic) | Fixed and often mid-dilated |
| **Response to pain** | None | May have subtle reflex | None (if deep coma) | None (but spinal reflexes may occur) |
| **ECG** | Asystole or PEA | J wave (Osborn wave), bradycardia | Sinus rhythm or other | Heart rate normal (e.g., sinus) |
| **Key differentiator** | No circulation, no brainstem function | Core temp <32°C + history of exposure | History + pinpoint pupils + bradypnoea + response to naloxone | Clinically brain dead but heart beating; requires specific testing protocol |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
- **Missed reversible cause (e.g., opioid overdose):** Patient incorrectly certified as dead → no naloxone given → actual death occurs. **Signs:** Pinpoint pupils, needle marks, shallow breathing. **Action:** Always check for drug history and pupil size. If in doubt, do not certify; start CPR and give naloxone if suspected.
- **Certification of a live person (very rare):** The patient later regains consciousness (e.g., after a seizure, hypoglycaemia). **Signs:** sudden movement, gasping, pulse returns. **Action:** immediate call for help, do not certify until fully evaluated. Document incident and senior review.
- **Incorrect cause of death on MCCD:** May lead to coroner's investigation, distressed family, or legal proceedings. **Prevention:** Always check medical records, discuss with senior if uncertain, and never guess.
- **Failure to refer to coroner when required:** Leads to delay, potential prosecution for concealing a death. **Examples:** Industrial disease, post-surgical death, suicide. **Action:** Know the list of coroner-notifiable deaths (e.g., all unnatural, sudden unexpected, within 24h of admission, during an operation).
- **Late-presentation pattern – the unexamined death:** If a patient is found dead after hours of being unwitnessed, signs of decomposition (greenish discoloration, bloating, putrid odour) are present. The examiner must still document signs of death but should not delay certification if clear. However, if there is any suspicion of foul play, do not touch the body further and contact police.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
- **Mnemonic for death verification steps: "DRS ABC" (modified)**
  - **D** – Danger (gloves, privacy, environment)
  - **R** – Response (vocal, painful stimuli)
  - **S** – Send for help if needed (but here you are confirming death)
  - **A** – Airway (check patency? No — not needed once death confirmed, but before confirmation you might clear obstruction if you suspect reversible)
  - **B** – Breathing (look, listen, feel for 1 min)
  - **C** – Circulation (palpate carotid/femoral for 30s)
  Add: **P** – Pupils (light), **R** – Reflexes (pain), **E** – ECG (if doubt)
- **Another mnemonic for what to document: "TIME-LIFE"**
  - **T** – Time of examination
  - **I** – Identity confirmed
  - **M** – Mechanism (signs of death)
  - **E** – Environment (privacy, dignity maintained)
  - **L** – Legal (coroner involved? )
  - **I** – Instructions (for funeral director)
  - **F** – Family informed
  - **E** – Education (offer conversation)
- **Clinical pearls:**
  - **The “30/1” rule:** Pulseless for 30s + apnoea for 1 min + fixed pupils = clinically dead. No need for EEG.
  - **Hot tip:** If you are unsure about pupil dilation, look for a corneal reflex. If absent, it’s a strong confirmatory sign (not always present in deep coma but reliable).
  - **Gasping:** Terminal gasps (agonal breathing) can occur for several minutes after cardiac arrest—this does NOT mean alive. Do not mistake for respiratory effort. Confirm with palpation of pulse; if absent, it’s a sign of death, not life.
  - **Brady-arrhythmia in hypothermia:** Do not attempt to verify death until rewarmed. The saying: “You are not dead until you are warm and dead.”
  - **Don't forget the pacemaker:** Always document presence or absence. If present, mortuary staff must deactivate it to avoid shocking staff.
  - **The “death certificate” is a legal document:** Write in permanent black ink, no correction fluid. If you make an error, draw a single line through the mistake and initial.
  - **Coroner referral checklist:** Unnatural, sudden (<24h from onset), unknown cause, during treatment, related to occupation, suicide, during pregnancy, or in custody. Always phone coroner's officer if unsure.
- **Common exam / ward mistakes:**
  - ❌ Writing "cardiac arrest" as 1a cause of death.
  - ❌ Forgetting to check for a micro-pacemaker (cardiac resynchronisation therapy) — leads to defibrillator shocks in mortuary.
  - ❌ Not documenting the time of death accurately (must match the time of verification, not time of last seen alive).
  - ❌ Certifying death without examining the patient (e.g., relying on nursing report alone — never do this).
  - ❌ Using terms like "old age" or "frailty" without a solid history — this is increasingly scrutinised.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior doctor (Registrar/Consultant) when:**
  - Cause of death is unclear (no previous diagnosis, no known history)
  - You suspect an unnatural cause (trauma, poisoning, suicide)
  - Patient died within 24 hours of admission to hospital
  - Patient died in surgery or recovery
  - Patient died while under anaesthesia
  - Patient was a prisoner or in police custody
  - Family disputes cause of death
  - You are uncertain about the completion of the MCCD
- **Call coroner (via bereavement officer or direct) when:**
  - Any of the above and not yet seen by a consultant; or the death is reportable by statute
  - If the patient had an industrial disease (e.g., asbestosis, coal worker's pneumoconiosis)
  - If the death is a suicide (suspected or confirmed)
  - If the death was violent or unnatural (fall, RTA, stabbing)
  - If the body is to be buried abroad (unless MCCD already accepted)
- **Specific thresholds for when to involve ICU/crash team:**
  - **NEWS2** – not directly applicable for dead patient, but if you are in doubt about verification (e.g., suspected reversible cause) and the patient has a score of ≥5, initiate resuscitation call.
  - **Lactate** – not part of death verification. Use clinical signs.
  - **GCS** – if any motor or eye response >2, do not verify. If GCS 3 with no brainstem reflexes, brainstem death testing may be appropriate (but on ICU).
  - **Caveat:** Never call a crash team for a patient who is clearly dead with irreversible signs (rigor mortis, algor mortis, dependent lividity) — that is inappropriate. But if in any doubt, err on the side of calling for help.
